senses.definition: to become calmer or less angry, usually after a period of heightened emotions or intensity

examples.x: After their heated argument, they needed some time to cool off and reflect on the situation.
Taking a walk in the fresh air helped him cool off and gain a clearer perspective on the issue.
The athlete took a break to cool off after a strenuous workout.
After the argument, we both needed some time to cool off before discussing the issue again.

senses.definition: to find excitement, pleasure, or satisfaction in a particular activity or experience

examples.x: Some people get off on extreme sports and the adrenaline rush.
He claimed to get off on the thrill of public speaking and addressing large crowds.

senses.definition: to waste time or engage in unproductive or silly activities instead of doing something more important or responsible

examples.x: She often goofs off at home when she should be doing chores.
They goofed off during the meeting and missed important information.

senses.definition: to assist someone in taking off a piece of clothing
to reach a stable or steady state after a period of fluctuation or change

examples.x: Sales have leveled off after a period of rapid growth, indicating a more sustainable pace of expansion.
The price of oil has leveled off after a period of sharp fluctuations, providing some relief to consumers.
The athlete's heart rate leveled off after the initial burst of exertion, settling into a sustainable pace.

senses.definition: to financially survive by depending on someone or something else

examples.x: The unemployed man is currently living off his savings until he finds a new job.
She has been able to live off her savings for the past year.
Many students live off their parents' support while attending university.
They live off the land, growing their own food and raising livestock.
He lives off the royalties from his successful book series.

senses.definition: (of an area) to be directly accessible from another area without having to pass through an intervening space

examples.x: The living room opens off the kitchen, creating a seamless flow between the two spaces.
The cafe opens off the bustling city square, attracting pedestrians with its outdoor seating.
The hallway opens off the main entrance, providing easy access to the bedrooms.
The balcony opens off the bedroom, providing a private outdoor retreat.
The dining area opens off the lounge, making it convenient for serving meals.

senses.definition: to make a short visit to a place on the way to another destination

examples.x: I'll stop off to see my grandparents on the way to the family reunion.
She decided to stop off and surprise her friend with a quick visit during her road trip.
We should stop off at the florist to buy flowers for the hostess before going to the dinner party.
He suggested stopping off at the market to pick up fresh produce for tonight's dinner.
On their way to the concert, they stopped off at a restaurant for dinner.

senses.definition: to transfer a responsibility, task, or authority to another person or party

examples.x: She handed off the leadership of the committee to her successor.
The CEO handed off the decision-making authority to the executive team.
She will hand off her coaching duties to the assistant coach for the upcoming game.

senses.definition: to release substances, energy, or elements into the surrounding environment

examples.x: The candles give off a warm and comforting glow.
The flowers give off a delightful fragrance in the garden.
When heated, this material gives off a distinct odor.
The factory gives off harmful pollutants into the atmosphere.
The electronic device gives off a low hum when it's turned on.
The flowers give off a pleasant fragrance in the garden.

senses.definition: to accompany someone to their point of departure and say goodbye to them

examples.x: The whole family gathered to see off their son as he left for military service.
We went to the airport to see off our friend who was flying to another country.
The school staff and students saw off their retiring principal with a heartfelt ceremony.

senses.definition: to dispose items or assets by selling them, often at discounted prices

examples.x: The store decided to sell off its old inventory at a clearance sale.
The company had to sell off some of its equipment to cover the losses.
They had to sell off their family heirlooms to pay for medical bills.
During the garage sale, they're planning to sell off their unused furniture and appliances.

senses.definition: to transfer someone to a different location or destination
to unintentionally fall asleep, especially for a short period

examples.x: After a long day at work, he found himself dozing off in the cozy armchair.
The soothing rhythm of the rain outside the window made her doze off during the afternoon.
While reading a book in bed, she gradually dozed off, the novel slipping from her hands.
During the boring lecture, some students couldn't help but doze off at their desks.
The gentle rocking of the train and the soft hum of the engine made the passenger doze off on the journey.

senses.definition: to fall asleep, often unintentionally or unexpectedly

examples.x: After a long day at work, she found herself starting to drop off on the couch.
The soothing rhythm of the train made him drop off during the journey.
While reading a book in bed, I tend to drop off after a few pages.
The monotonous lecture caused some students to drop off in the middle of the class.
As the airplane engines hummed, passengers began to drop off for a mid-flight nap.

senses.definition: to unintentionally fall asleep for a short period of time, especially while sitting up

examples.x: I tend to nod off during long meetings.
Despite my efforts to stay awake, I nodded off during the lecture.
The baby is nodding off in the stroller.
By the time the plane took off, I had already nodded off.
Sometimes, people nod off while watching a boring movie.

senses.definition: to recover from the effects of something, such as fatigue or illness, through sleeping

examples.x: They decided to sleep off the fatigue from the long hike in the mountains.
The athlete needed to sleep off the muscle soreness from the intense training session.
I usually sleep off a cold by resting and getting plenty of sleep.

senses.definition: to take the best or most profitable part of something, leaving the rest for others

examples.x: The corrupt official was accused of creaming off funds from the public project for personal gain.
Some companies are known to cream off top talent from their competitors by offering attractive incentives.
The chef skillfully creamed off the richest flavors for the signature dish, leaving a delightful culinary experience.
The manager decided to cream off the most talented employees for the prestigious project, recognizing their skills and expertise.

senses.definition: to act in a way that is intended to impress others

examples.x: The chef showed off by creating an intricate dessert.
He was showing off by lifting the heaviest weights at the gym.
The student kept showing off by answering all the questions before anyone else had a chance.
He took every opportunity to show off.
She showed off her new dress at the party.

senses.definition: to make someone feel extremely angry or annoyed
to intimidate and frighten someone

examples.x: The persistent rumors scared off potential investors.

senses.definition: to anger or frustrate someone by one's actions or behaviors

examples.x: His constant interruptions during the meeting really ticked off his colleagues.
The careless remarks made by the speaker managed to tick off several audience members.

senses.definition: to call out numbers in order, usually for organizational purposes or to determine positions

examples.x: In preparation for the field trip, the teacher asked the students to count off to ensure everyone had a buddy.
During the rehearsal, the director had the actors count off to assign roles for the upcoming play.
The coach instructed the players to count off for their assigned positions on the field.
To organize the queue, the event staff asked attendees to count off before entering the venue.

senses.definition: to read items from a list

examples.x: The teacher asked each student to read off their favorite book from a list.
She read off the names of the prize winners at the event.
During the presentation, she will read off the key points on the slides.

senses.definition: to measure a distance by counting the number of steps taken

examples.x: The architect asked the surveyor to step off the dimensions of the building site.
The farmer instructed his workers to step off the distance for planting each row of crops.
In the absence of a measuring tape, the explorer had to step off the length of the cave passage.
During the camping trip, they had to step off the space for setting up each tent in the designated area.

senses.definition: to speak loudly or complain, often in a bold or confrontational manner

examples.x: The employee was mouthing off at the manager during the heated argument.
He tends to mouth off about politics, expressing his views passionately.
During the game, the coach was mouthing off at the referee for making a questionable call.
While waiting in line, they are mouthing off about the service quality.
She often mouths off when she disagrees with a decision.

senses.definition: to express strong and often negative opinions about something, typically in a rude manner

examples.x: The coach sounded off in the locker room, motivating the team with a forceful and impassioned speech.
He tends to sound off at meetings, making his strong opinions on the matter abundantly clear.
The political activist sounded off during the rally, passionately expressing their views on social justice.
During the heated discussion, he constantly sounded off, dominating the conversation with his viewpoints.
The professor sounded off in the lecture hall, challenging students to think critically about the topic.

senses.definition: to express sharp disapproval or criticism of someone's behavior or actions

examples.x: He was told off by his manager for missing the deadline.

senses.definition: to share an idea with someone and get their thoughts or opinions

examples.x: Bouncing off your goals with a friend can provide valuable insights.
The manager bounced off the proposal during the team meeting.
Let's bounce off these marketing strategies to see which one works best.

senses.definition: to record one's departure or the end of one's work shift using a timekeeping system, often involving the use of a clock or electronic device

examples.x: When the workday is over, employees need to clock off to ensure accurate attendance records.
Don't forget to clock off before leaving to make sure your hours are properly accounted for.
The automated system allows employees to easily clock off at the end of their shifts.
The workers eagerly line up at the time clock to clock off and head home.
It's important to remember to clock off on time to maintain a healthy work-life balance.

senses.definition: to recite information without hesitation and fluently

examples.x: During the interview, he was able to reel off his qualifications and experience without hesitation.
She can effortlessly reel off all the U.S. state capitals in alphabetical order.
The auctioneer reeled off the items for sale one by one, keeping the crowd engaged.

senses.definition: to discreetly share important information or advice with someone to help them take action or avoid a problem

examples.x: Let's tip off the boss about the potential issues with the project.
He didn't want to tip off his competitors about the new product launch.
The spy needed to tip off headquarters about the enemy's plans.

senses.definition: to become less severe, intense, or harsh

examples.x: As the storm moved away, the winds began to ease off, and the rain subsided.
The doctor assured the patient that the pain would gradually ease off with proper medication.
The intense heat started to ease off in the evening as the sun set.
As tensions in the room escalated, the mediator worked to create an environment where emotions could ease off.
The teacher noticed the students' anxiety easing off as they gained confidence in the subject.

senses.definition: to decrease in quantity, intensity, or level over time

examples.x: The excitement of the event began to tail off towards the end.
The effectiveness of the medication might tail off over time.
The crowd's enthusiasm tailed off as the performance went on.
Motivation can tail off if the goals are not clear.

senses.definition: to slowly get quieter and eventually stop

examples.x: The train's whistle trailed off as it disappeared around the bend.
The fireworks trailed off, leaving only the echoes in the night.
The hum of conversation in the cafe trailed off with the closing hours.
The engine noise of the car trailed off as it moved farther away.

senses.definition: to gradually fade in color or quality over time due to constant use or other factors

examples.x: After a while, the paint on the playground equipment began to wear off.
The lettering on the street signs started to wear off due to exposure to the elements.
The polish on the wooden furniture tends to wear off with regular cleaning.
The charm of the antique mirror began to wear off as its silver coating faded.
Over time, the vibrant colors on the poster started to wear off.
After years of wearing, the intricate design on the watch had been completely worn off.

senses.definition: to actively make effort to make something disappear

examples.x: We need to work off the negative perceptions by demonstrating improvement.
She is determined to work off the procrastination habits through a structured plan.
The team is collectively working off the challenges of the complex project.
The meditation sessions are effective in working off mental exhaustion.

senses.definition: to casually ignore something or someone

examples.x: The professor brushed off the student's question during the lecture.
Despite the warnings, she casually brushed off the potential risks.

senses.definition: to make something seem less serious by joking about it

examples.x: The politician laughed off the accusations of corruption, saying that they were nothing more than a smear campaign.
The athlete laughed off the injury, saying that it was just a minor setback.
The student laughed off the bad grade, saying that it was just one test and it didn't matter.

senses.definition: to consider something unworthy of one's attention or consideration

examples.x: The manager advised the team to shrug off the distractions and maintain productivity.
Despite the challenges, they managed to shrug off the setbacks and move forward.
The athlete decided to shrug off the negative comments and stay focused on training.

senses.definition: to consider someone or something as having no value or importance

examples.x: Despite our high hopes, we had to write off the initiative as unsuccessful.
The committee had no choice but to write off the experiment as a setback.
After working for six months, we eventually wrote off the project as a non-starter.
After several unsuccessful attempts, they wrote off the idea as unfeasible.

senses.definition: to gain strength from a specific source or influence

examples.x: Rumors feed off people's curiosity and spread quickly through the office.
The mob's anger fed off the escalating tensions in the community.
Her ambition feeds off the challenges she faces, making her more determined to succeed.
Misinformation often feeds off ignorance, growing more pervasive when unchecked.
